subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are the critical temperatures of some of the gases:

Gas Critical temperature K
He 5.2
CH4 190
CO2 304.2
NH3 405.5

The gas showing least adsorption on a definite amount of charcoal is
question:
Given below are the critical temperatures of some of the gases:

Gas Critical temperature K
He 5.2
CH4 190
CO2 304.2
NH3 405.5

The gas showing least adsorption on a definite amount of charcoal is
solution:
The amount of gas adsorbed by a solid depends on the nature of gas. In general, easily liquefiable gases (i.e., with higher critical temperatures) are readily adsorbed as van der Waals’ forces are stronger near the critical temperatures. Thus, 1g of activated charcoal adsorbs more sulphur dioxide (critical temperature 630K), than methane (critical temperature 190K) which is still more than 4.5 mL of dihydrogen (critical temperature 33K).

So, more the critical temperature of gas greater is the ease of liquefaction hence greater is the adsorption.

Hence, among the given gases ammonia is adsorbed most and helium the least on definite amount of charcoal.

q:
Adding surfactants in non polar solvent, the micelles structure will look like

(a)

(b)

(c)

 

(d)
question:
Adding surfactants in non polar solvent, the micelles structure will look like

(a)

(b)

(c)

 

(d)
solution:
Micelles are formed by self-assembly of amphiphilic molecules. The structures contain hydrophilic/polar region (head) and hydrophobic/nonpolar region (tail). Micelles are formed in aqueous solution whereby the polar region faces the outside surface of the micelle and the nonpolar region forms the core.

Non-polar part will interact with non-polar solvent.

q:
2.0 g of H2 gas is adsorbed on 2.5 g of platinum powder at 300 K and 1 bar pressure. The volume of the gas adsorbed per gram of the adsorbent is mL

(Given : R=0.083 L bar K-1 mol-1)
question:
2.0 g of H2 gas is adsorbed on 2.5 g of platinum powder at 300 K and 1 bar pressure. The volume of the gas adsorbed per gram of the adsorbent is mL

(Given : R=0.083 L bar K-1 mol-1)
solution:
VH2 = nRTPVH2g=220.0833001

∴ Volume of H2g adsorbed on 2.5 Pt=24.9 L

∴ Volume of H2g adsorbed on 1 g of Pt=24.92.5=9.96 L=9960 ml

q:
The number of incorrect statement/s from the following is/are

A. Water vapours are adsorbed by anhydrous calcium chloride.

B. There is a decrease in surface energy during adsorption.

C. As the adsorption proceeds, ΔH becomes more and more negative.

D. Adsorption is accompanied by decrease in entropy of the system.
question:
The number of incorrect statement/s from the following is/are

A. Water vapours are adsorbed by anhydrous calcium chloride.

B. There is a decrease in surface energy during adsorption.

C. As the adsorption proceeds, ΔH becomes more and more negative.

D. Adsorption is accompanied by decrease in entropy of the system.
solution: Water vapours are absorbed by anhydrous calcium chloride. There is a decrease in surface energy during adsorption. As the adsorption proceeds, ΔH becomes more and more negative. Adsorption is accompanied by decrease in entropy of the system. Thus, option A and C are incorrect.

subject: Chemistry
q: In Freundlich adsorption isotherm, slope of AB line is:
question: In Freundlich adsorption isotherm, slope of AB line is:
solution:
Freundlich adsorption isotherm is :

xm=kp1/n

x= mass of adsorbate
m= mass of adsorbent
P= eq. pressure

k1n=1nlogp+logk
y=mx+c

compairing

m=1n=slope 1n=0 to 1

n>1

logxm=logK+1nlogP

y=c+mx

m=1/n so slope will be equal to 1/n

Hence 0≤1n≤1

question:

(B)

(C)

solution:
The equation xm=KP1/n represents Freundlich adsorption isotherm. It is an empirical relationship between the amount of gas adsorbed by a given amount of solid adsorbent surface and pressure of the gas at a particular temperature.

So, Freundlich adsorption isotherm equation is

xm=KP1/n

⇒logxm=logK+1nlogP

Extent of adsorption xm decreases with increase in temperature.

With the help of the above two equation, following plots are obtained

subject: Chemistry
q:
Based on the given figure, the number of correct statement/s is/are

A. Surface tension is the outcome of equal attractive and repulsion forces acting on the liquid molecule in bulk.

B. Surface tension is due to uneven forces acting on the molecules present on the surface.

C. The molecule in the bulk can never come to the liquid surface.

D. The molecules on the surface are responsible for vapour pressure if the system is a closed system.
question:
Based on the given figure, the number of correct statement/s is/are

A. Surface tension is the outcome of equal attractive and repulsion forces acting on the liquid molecule in bulk.

B. Surface tension is due to uneven forces acting on the molecules present on the surface.

C. The molecule in the bulk can never come to the liquid surface.

D. The molecules on the surface are responsible for vapour pressure if the system is a closed system.
solution:
Surface tension is the outcome of unequal attractive and repulsion forces acting on the liquid molecule in bulk. Surface tension is due to uneven forces acting on the molecules present on the surface. The molecule in the bulk can come to the liquid surface- due to brownian movement and convection. The molecules on the surface are responsible for vapour pressure if the system is a closed system.

Thus, B and D options are correct.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The flocculation value of HCl for arsenic sulphide sol is 30 mmolL-1. If H2SO4 is used for the flocculation of arsenic sulphide, the amount, in grams, of H2SO4 in 250ml required for the above purpose is ________
(molecular mass of H2SO4=98 g/mol
question:
The flocculation value of HCl for arsenic sulphide sol is 30 mmolL-1. If H2SO4 is used for the flocculation of arsenic sulphide, the amount, in grams, of H2SO4 in 250ml required for the above purpose is ________
(molecular mass of H2SO4=98 g/mol
solution:
For 1L sol 30 mmol of HCl is required
∴ For 1L sol 15 mmol H2SO4 is required
For 250 mL of sol 154×10-3 mmol H2SO4

Mass of H2SO4=3.75 × 10-3×98 = 0.3675 g

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : Emulsions of oil in water are unstable and sometimes they separate into two layers on standing.

Statement II : For stabilisation of an emulsion, excess of electrolyte is added.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : Emulsions of oil in water are unstable and sometimes they separate into two layers on standing.

Statement II : For stabilisation of an emulsion, excess of electrolyte is added.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.
solution:
Oil and water are not soluble in each other. since oils are non-polar organic compounds and water is a polar compound. So, they will form hydrophobic type of colloid.

Hydrophobic collides are unstable and required some stabilisers like proteins, gums, natural and synthetic soaps, etc. Excess of electrolyte can not act as stabilisers.

So, statement 1 is true but statement 2 is false.

q: Adsorption of a gas follows Freundlich adsorption isotherm. In the given plot, x is the mass of the gas adsorbed on mass m of the adsorbent at pressure P . xm is proportional to:
question: Adsorption of a gas follows Freundlich adsorption isotherm. In the given plot, x is the mass of the gas adsorbed on mass m of the adsorbent at pressure P . xm is proportional to:
solution:
According to the Freundlich adsorption isotherm, xm=Kp1n

Taking log on both sides.

logxm=logK+1nlog⁡P

Slope from graph =y2-y1x2-x1=24=12 or, 1n=12

∴  xm=Kp12

or xm∝p12

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is used for the preparation of colloids ?
question: Which of the following is used for the preparation of colloids ?
solution:
Bredig’s Arc Method is used for preparation of colloidal sol’s of less reactive metal like Au, Ag, Pt.

Ostwald process is used for preparation of nitric acid.

Van Arkel method is used for refining Zirconium or Titanium.

The Mond process is a method for refining nickel. The nickel oxide and impurities react with the hydrogen gas to form an impure solid nickel.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List I with List II

List I List II
A Physisorption I Single Layer Adsorption
B Chemisorption II 20-40 kJ mol-1
C N2g+3H2g⟶Fe(s)2NH3g III Chromatography
D Analytical Application or Adsorption IV Heterogeneous catalysis

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List I with List II

List I List II
A Physisorption I Single Layer Adsorption
B Chemisorption II 20-40 kJ mol-1
C N2g+3H2g⟶Fe(s)2NH3g III Chromatography
D Analytical Application or Adsorption IV Heterogeneous catalysis

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) In physisorption, the enthalpy heat is low and lies between 20 - 40 kJ mol-1. In physisorption, multilayers of adsorbed particles are formed.

(B) In chemisorption, the enthalpy heat is high and lies within the range of 40 - 200 kJ mol-1.  In chemisorption, a monolayer of adsorbed particles is formed.

(C) N2g+3H2g⟶Fe(s)2NH3g

Heterogeneous catalysts occupy a different phase. Generally, heterogeneous catalysts are solid compounds that are added to liquid or gas reaction mixtures. Fe is used as a heterogeneous catalyst in the Haber process.

(D) In the process of adsorption chromatography, different compounds are adsorbed on the adsorbent to different degrees based on the absorptivity of the component. 

So, the correct matching is A-IV, B-II, C-III, D-I.

question:

R=0.0821 L atm K-1 mol-1
solution:
V=3.1232×0.0821×3001=2.40 L

∵ 1.2 gm adsorbs 2.40 L

∴ 1 gm adsorbs 2 L

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> in water was prepared in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> beaker. Albumin from the egg was poured into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> solution and stirred well. This resulted in a/ an
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> in water was prepared in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> beaker. Albumin from the egg was poured into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> solution and stirred well. This resulted in a/ an
solution:
The most appropriate method of making egg-albumin sol is Break an egg carefully and transfer the transparent part of the content to 100 mL of 5% w/V saline solution and stir well. Since the egg-albumin sol can be prepared by stirring only so, it is a lyophilic sol. Since lyophilic sols are easy to prepare while lyophobic sols required some spacial techniques.

Note: egg-albumin is the transparent part of egg. During preparation of sol, water should be cold. If we boil water, the sol will be coagulated.

q: In figure, a straight line is given for Freundrich Adsorption (y=3x+2.505). The value of 1n and logK are respectively.
question: In figure, a straight line is given for Freundrich Adsorption (y=3x+2.505). The value of 1n and logK are respectively.
solution:
The expression for the Freundlich isotherm can be represented by the following equation

xm=KpP1/n

Taking log on both sides

logxm=logk+1nlogP

On comparing with given equation of straight line, we get

Y=3x+2.505, 1n=3, logK=2.505

So, the answer is 3 and 2.505

subject: Chemistry
q:
CH4 is adsorbed on 1 g charcoal at 0°C following the Freundlich adsorption isotherm. 10.0 mL of CH4 is adsorbed at 100 mm of Hg, whereas 15.0 mL is adsorbed at 200 mm of Hg. The volume of CH4 adsorbed at 300 mm of Hg is 10x mL. The value of x is _________ ×10-2. (Nearest integer)
[Use log102=0.3010, log103=0.4771]
question:
CH4 is adsorbed on 1 g charcoal at 0°C following the Freundlich adsorption isotherm. 10.0 mL of CH4 is adsorbed at 100 mm of Hg, whereas 15.0 mL is adsorbed at 200 mm of Hg. The volume of CH4 adsorbed at 300 mm of Hg is 10x mL. The value of x is _________ ×10-2. (Nearest integer)
[Use log102=0.3010, log103=0.4771]
solution:
xm=Kp1n

x∝V for the same gas
101=K1001n   ...(1)
151=K2001n   ....(2)
V1=K3001n   ...(3)
Divide (2) to (1) =1510=21n
log32=1nlog2
1n=0.4771-0.30100.3010=0.585
Divide (3) to (1) =V10=31n
logV10=1nlog3
logV10=0.585×0.4771=0.2791
V10=100.2791
V=10×100.2791=101.2791=10x
x=1.2791=127.91×10-2≈128×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q: The hydrocarbon which can react with sodium in liquid ammonia is
question: The hydrocarbon which can react with sodium in liquid ammonia is
solution:
$$
\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{CH} \stackrel{\mathrm{Na} / \mathrm{Liq} 9 \mathrm{NH}}{\Delta} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{C} \equiv \stackrel{\ominus}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{Na}^{\oplus}
$$
It is a terminal alkyne, having acidic hydrogen.
Note: Solve it as a case of terminal alkynes, otherwise all alkynes react with $\mathrm{Na}$ in liq. $\mathrm{NH}_3$.
